posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked:
Oh how I love Chassagne Rouge. I don’t know why it strikes the right notes for me.

Tonight it is a 2017 JC Ramonet Chassagne Rouge Boudriotte. After a little decant, this just oozes bright berry notes. Firm mineral on the palate and so precise on the mid palate. The finish is clean. Close to my favorite which is the 2017 JC Ramonet Chassagne Clos St Jean which is absolute magic and aromatically my absolute favorite Chassagne Rouge from my 2017 collection.

I often pick this over any of my other wines. So satisfying. Precise. The epitome of Burgundy in many ways.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: Reminded me I didn’t have any of the 17 CSJ… picked up a 6 pack.

posts.name: Jonathan Grunzweig
posts.cooked:
Below is my TN on a lovely 1998 Ramonet Chassagne Montrachet I enjoyed a few months ago. If you like, you can add ageability to your list of praiseworthy attributed IMO, thanks:

Such a fascinating Bastille Day treat, served alongside (and really outshining) the much better known Ramonet Blanc from the same vintage. Obscure enough that mine is apparently the first and only TN on CellarTracker. A wine more about the aromatics than anything else, with a complex and evolving array of perfumed, spiced and smoky notes. I detected tar, licorice and even some black pepper, likely would have called it Nebbiolo had I been blinded. The wine itself somewhat lacked concentration, presumably a reflection of the vintage, but it did evidence impressive structure and tannins, leading us to credit presumed stem-inclusion (but I don’t have actual insight on the winemaking).

–Jonathan

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
William. You are a lucky man to have that wine. I can only imagine. They really do age great especially that bottling (Clos de la Boudriotte) from Ramonet. Cheers.

My tasting note (given that the wine is very far from being on the market, I can’t think any of our subscribers will object to my sharing pre-publication teaser):

A magical bottle at the peak of its powers, Ramonet’s 1979 Chassagne-Montrachet 1er Cru Clos de la Boudriotte bursts with aromas of peonies, spices, orange rind, red berries, musk and forest floor. Medium to full-bodied, supple and velvety, its fleshy core of fruit is framed by melting tannins and succulent acids, concluding with a sapid, expansive finish.

I don’t know what’s planted there but I would speculate the selections of Pinot Noir are quite interesting, given the recurring perfumed quality the wines reliably display!

In fact, I don’t think there is any relation, certainly not a close one, though I may be misremembering.

Jean-François Germain makes the Henri Germain wines, and his brother Eric has been brining a lot of Germain magic to the Vincent Girardin wines. It’s just hard to get people to put their preconceptions about that label aside and taste the wines for what they are. Eric is very well respected along the Côte for a reason!

I buy and drink Ramonet and Niellon consistently, what are thoughts on other producers like Colin, Maltroye, Drouhin, Bouchard, Latour, Girardin, Morey-Coffinet, Amiot, and Gagnard.

It has been a long time, but when I did dabble on a set of CM Rouge, I had the Maltroye in the mix and liked the wines, but they showed their wood in youth. Beware. The Drouhin is on my shelf now and I’ve been enjoying it, but not at the same level as Ramonet; I do think it was priced lower than Ramonet and is village-level, to be fair.

Cheers,
fred

Well, you win some fliers and you lose some! The rest of the case will make great gifts!

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
I had two Ramonet reds on Thursday night. The first was a very beautiful 2017 Jean-Claude Ramonet Chassagne-Montrachet Rouge. This village wine was just beautiful to drink. Very elegant and pure. Probably will age but nothing wrong with drinking it now.

The second wine was a 2012 Noël Ramonet Chassagne-Montrachet La Boudriotte Rouge. This was a more serious wine, but with the same range of beautiful red fruit and spicy flavors. Really not yet mature, the wine had a long finish and well was just wonderful. As I understand it, this wine was actually made by Jean-Claude Ramonet, the brother of Noël Ramonet. Many years of life left for this wine.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: In addition to Chassagne reds, we should really talk about Beaune reds more. Over the last two weeks, I’ve had bottles of Bouley’s Reversées and David Croix’s Tulivans that have been utterly delicious. It is an interesting AOC, as it is separated from Pommard and Savigny by two combes, and bisected by two more (cf. e.g. Pommard which is bisected by only one, and shares another with Beaune on its northern border). This means a lot of complexity in terms of geology (alluvial fans from the combes) and nuances in terms of water flow and air flow down from the combes. It’s also simply a very big AOC with lots of variation of altitude and exposition. And then, the climats are very big indeed, to the extent of being almost useless: between the bottom of Les Aigrots and the Clos des Aigrots up slope, for example, there is a world of difference; the same can be said with the bottom and top of Grèves, Teurons, etc. There is an immensely bright future for Beaune, I tend to think!

posts.name: Michael S. Monie
posts.cooked: I just had Drouhin’s 2019 “Côte de Beaune” as opposed to the bottling that includes villages. The front label has the same script used on Mouches. Very solid wine at its price point.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I just had Drouhin’s 2019 “Côte de Beaune” as opposed to the bottling that includes villages. The front label has the same script used on Mouches. Very solid wine at its price point.

Yes, the 2019 turned out especially well. IIRC it also contains declassified young vine 1er cru fruit.

The AOC “Côte de Beaune” itself is very interesting, as there are some top sites such as Pierres Blanches that are not at all know. Sadly, as a result, the viticulture is not always on the level that it would need to be to deliver greatness, but the soils and exposition are immensely promising.

posts.name: Keith Levenberg
posts.cooked: Lots of great Beaune. The top 1er crus are devalued because there are too many 1er crus that probably shouldn’t be.

posts.name: Glen Gold

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Lots of great Beaune. The top 1er crus are devalued because there are too many 1er crus that probably shouldn’t be.

One hears that said, but I think it’s more a question of the producers of Beaune frequently being being houses who have historically treated them as, for the most part, “entry level” 1er crus, farming and vinifying them accordingly. Some Beaune 1ers are clearly better than others, don’t get me wrong, but I don’t see all that many that ought to be downgraded (and this is where I walk on my weekends, in addition to farming a small parcel, so I know the lay of the ground pretty well at this stage). On the other hand, I would certainly divide them up into more meaningful climats, because those that we have are simply too big in many cases.

posts.name: Pat_Martin
posts.cooked:
As one relatively new to red burgundy appreciation, how do Chassagne reds compare stylistically to other villages?

FWIW, I tend to put Chambolle and Volnay on the smoother, richer end of things and Pommard on the other sterner, firmer side. (I’m sure such a broad generalization has numerous exceptions, but that’s just my loose sense of things).

FWIW, I tend to put Chambolle and Volnay on the smoother, richer end of things and Pommard on the other sterner, firmer side. (I’m sure such a broad generalization has numerous exceptions, but that’s just my loose sense of things).

I mostly drink two producers - Ramonet and Bernard Moreau.

I find Ramonet’s reds to be very red fruited and somewhat spicy. Not a terribly rich wine but very elegant, long and complex.

I have a hard time answering your question because I find Moreau’s wine very different, although also very tasting. I find them to have darker fruits and not to be as spicy, but are still long and complex. Unfortunately, I have only had his Cardeuse from barrel samples, but fortunately that will change.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
As one relatively new to red burgundy appreciation, how do Chassagne reds compare stylistically to other villages?

FWIW, I tend to put Chambolle and Volnay on the smoother, richer end of things and Pommard on the other sterner, firmer side. (I’m sure such a broad generalization has numerous exceptions, but that’s just my loose sense of things).

In the 20th century, they have tended to be quite chunky, rich, dark fruited and somewhat austere, rustic wines; but it is the exceptions to this rule that are the interesting wines!

Chassagne has some superb sites for the production of authoritative but quite elegant red wine, so the reason for the wines’ historic rusticity has more to do with reds made from some of the lesser sites; and, even more so, poor quality selections of Pinot Noir genetics. Marsannay, Pernand and Santenay were the sources of very productive selections of Pinot in the later 19th century, and Chassagne’s proximity to Santenay meant that many growers adopted these fatter-berried, higher yielding selections. In the 1970s, very productive SO4 rootstocks came into the picture, too. These selections’ tendency to produce huge yields is one reason Chassagne growers traditionally pruned in cordon rather than Guyot - as a way to mitigate the yields. Here’s a photo of the grapes that such selections produce that I took during the 2020 harvest:


Chassagne Pinot Noir by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr

Grapes like this produce wine that’s somewhat herbaceous and rustic, with chewy tannins. And by the looks of it, these vines seemed to me to be around forty years old, so vine age only does so much in terms of mitigating productivity. Of course, the vine’s phenotypic expression can be modulated by viticulture: increase the height of the trellising and don’t hedge, and clusters will elongate and berries become smaller. But if you farm using herbicide, lots of nitrogen fertilizer, and regular 1.10 meter trimming, you are not going to have raw materials conducive to producing great wine. And this would and does hold just as true of vines like these growing in a top Vosne-Romanée 1er cru as it does in Chassagne.

One upside to the decline of Chassagne red is that the least suitable sites and worst quality vine material has tended to be ripped out first, leaving us with the cream that floated to the top. Sites such as Ramonet’s Clos de la Boudriotte are planted with quite fine selections of Pinot Noir and it’s not surprising that the wines are more elegant, perfumed, and sensual. It is therefore the appellation’s best examples that give us a sense of what is possible with Chassagne red (I’d be tempted to agree with Rodier, who thought them the most Côte de Nuits-like of the Côte de Beaune’s reds, for what little such generalizations are worth), rather than the average.

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
In addition to Chassagne reds, we should really talk about Beaune reds more. Over the last two weeks, I’ve had bottles of Bouley’s Reversées and David Croix’s Tulivans that have been utterly delicious. It is an interesting AOC, as it is separated from Pommard and Savigny by two combes, and bisected by two more (cf. e.g. Pommard which is bisected by only one, and shares another with Beaune on its northern border). This means a lot of complexity in terms of geology (alluvial fans from the combes) and nuances in terms of water flow and air flow down from the combes. It’s also simply a very big AOC with lots of variation of altitude and exposition. And then, the climats are very big indeed, to the extent of being almost useless: between the bottom of Les Aigrots and the Clos des Aigrots up slope, for example, there is a world of difference; the same can be said with the bottom and top of Grèves, Teurons, etc. There is an immensely bright future for Beaune, I tend to think!

****David Croix’s Tulivans that have been utterly delicious. ****

Kind of late joining this thread. I agree that Davi Corix’s reds are utterly delicious. Sadly only 4 of them are available in Quebec Canada. and they are as follows :

Des Croix -Beaune 1er - Les Cents Vignes
Des Croix -A-Corton Les Boutieres

Pretty disappointed in a Germain CM red last night. Anyone experienced a similar green streak?

  • 2018 Henri Germain et Fils Chassagne-Montrachet Rouge - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Chassagne-Montrachet (11/27/2021)
    Double decant an hour before serving. Finally opened up to reveal crunchy red fruit on the nose. Lovely, beguiling nose. Palate was more reserved with a mean green note on the finish. Odd and unexpected for this warm and full vintage. Maybe it will integrate with more time.

posts.name: Troy Stark
posts.cooked:
Any thoughts on the specific vineyards?

In my long but limited experience, I’ve found Boudriottes consistently fine from several producers, I’d have to say my favorite (but I haven’t had that many). Clos St Jean? Maltroie? Any suggestions?

Dan Kravitz

I would add La Cardeuse from Morgeot. Other parts of Morgeot.

Yes, Morgeot is great for Pinot. Relatively less great for Chard. My favorites lieu dits in Morgeot have been mentioned; Boudriottes and La Cardeuse.

That said, Clos St. Jean, especially the old vines owned by the Pillots, often reaches GC quality, IMHO. Unlike Morgeot, both reds and whites from the Clos can be amazing due to the incredibly high chalk content of the soil.

Thierry does all his Clos Saint-Jean with “dressage”, so no hedging of the canopies, and ferments with some whole bunch. posts.name: Wil Raley
posts.cooked:
image1920×2560 203 KB

I was inspired by this thread so I tracked down some Ramonet Rouge and popped the cork today. Just a Village level, but the wine was stunning. Very light in color, almost rose like. Nose was a little oak dominant to start but that integrated with air and gave way to holiday spice and a cool mineral breeze. The palate was a parade of pretty red fruit, with sexy oak that needs to integrate just a bit more, and tannin to go the distance. Such a beautiful village wine that has a lot left in the tank. Definitely gonna step up to the CSJ or Boudriotte next.

Had my first Moreau Cardeuse tonight, a very lovely, elegant, and aromatic 2017 that didn’t have nearly the density of the handful of red Pillot Chassagne I’ve had. This had a bit more of a tannic line than the Ramonet reds, but the balance fruit and elegance reminded me of them. I’m guessing it’s some function of the vintage character, and wondering if the '18s and '19s would swing the other way.

BTW this was a ridiculous deal at Envoyer, just 50 bucks a bottle, but I only received a fraction of what I requested.

posts.name: SeanSydney
posts.cooked:
My note on a 2018 Goujonne I popped to take a look at earlier in the year:

What I love about this wine, like most Chassagne reds I’ve encountered, is its elegance; yes, it’s still 2018 and thus is round and fleshy, but it’s supremely refined. Profound floral character, a touch of spice, and deep dark red fruits all come together wonderfully. Beautiful animating acidity, and an already-silky texture with just enough grippy, powdery tannin.
